Hands-on Exercise 4

Author

Lei Yuhe

Published

May 3, 2026

Modified

May 3, 2026

Visualising Distribution

Overview

Two new statistical graphic methods for visualizing distribution will be covered in this section.

  1. Ridgeline plots: Show distributions for multiple groups stacked along a shared axis, using ggridges.
  2. Raincloud plots: Combine a half-density, boxplot, and dot plot into one layered graphic, using ggdist.

Both reveal richer distributional shape such as skewness, multimodality, and tail behaviour.

Setting Up

pacman::p_load(ggdist, ggridges, ggthemes,
               colorspace, tidyverse)
Package Role
ggridges Ridgeline plots via geom_density_ridges() and variants
ggdist Half-eye, dot plots, uncertainty visualisation
ggthemes Extra themes, e.g. theme_economist() for publication-ready look
colorspace Colour manipulation via lighten(), darken(), palette tools
tidyverse Data wrangling (dplyr, tidyr), and base plotting (ggplot2)

Importing Data

exam <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")
glimpse(exam)
Rows: 322
Columns: 7
$ ID      <chr> "Student321", "Student305", "Student289", "Student227", "Stude…
$ CLASS   <chr> "3I", "3I", "3H", "3F", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3I", "3H", "3…
$ GENDER  <chr> "Male", "Female", "Male", "Male", "Male", "Female", "Male", "M…
$ RACE    <chr> "Malay", "Malay", "Chinese", "Chinese", "Malay", "Malay", "Chi…
$ ENGLISH <dbl> 21, 24, 26, 27, 27, 31, 31, 31, 33, 34, 34, 36, 36, 36, 37, 38…
$ MATHS   <dbl> 9, 22, 16, 77, 11, 16, 21, 18, 19, 49, 39, 35, 23, 36, 49, 30,…
$ SCIENCE <dbl> 15, 16, 16, 31, 25, 16, 25, 27, 15, 37, 42, 22, 32, 36, 35, 45…

The dataset has 322 students across 9 classes (3A–3I) with scores in English, Maths, and Science. Variables CLASS, GENDER, and RACE serve as grouping factors throughout this chapter.

Visualising Distribution with Ridgeline Plot

A ridgeline plot (formerly called a Joyplot) shows the distribution of a numeric variable for several groups, stacked with a slight overlap along the y-axis.

When to use ridgeline plots:

  • 5 or more groups to compare (if we have fewer groups, use violin/boxplot instead)
  • Existence of natural ordering to the groups (e.g. class ranking)
  • Reveal shape/distribution differences across groups, not just means

In the Exam dataset used here, 9 classes (3A–3I) are a perfect candidate.

Basic Ridgeline Plot

Distribution of English scores across all 9 classes using geom_density_ridges(). The classes are ordered 3A to 3I (best to weakest by class assignment).

  • The plot
  • The code
ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH,
           y = CLASS)) +
  geom_density_ridges(
    scale          = 3,
    rel_min_height = 0.01,
    bandwidth      = 3.4,
    fill           = lighten("#7097BB", .3),
    color          = "white"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name   = "English Score",
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(
    name   = NULL,
    expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))
  ) +
  theme_ridges()

Key arguments to understand:

  • scale = 3: It controls how much ridges overlap (higher = more overlap)
  • rel_min_height = 0.01: It trims tails below 1% of the peak height, avoiding long flat tails
  • bandwidth = 3.4: It controls the kernel density smoother width. The higher, the smoother.

Takeaway: Class 3A and 3B have distributions clustered at high scores (70-90), while 3H and 3I are concentrated at low scores (20-50). Notably, the shape differs as well, 3I shows a narrow, peaked distribution (homogeneously weak), while middle classes like 3E-3F show broader, flatter distributions indicating mixed ability.

Varying fill colors along the x axis

Instead of a single flat fill colour, we can let the fill vary along the score axis using geom_density_ridges_gradient(). This adds a visual cue for score magnitude.

  • The plot
  • The code
ggplot(exam,
       aes(x    = MATHS,
           y    = CLASS,
           fill = after_stat(x))) +
  geom_density_ridges_gradient(
    scale          = 3,
    rel_min_height = 0.01
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    name   = "Maths Score",
    option = "C"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name   = "Maths Score",
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(
    name   = NULL,
    expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))
  ) +
  theme_ridges()

Takeaway: The gradient fill immediately communicates which classes cluster at high vs low scores without needing to read axis values carefully. However, fill and alpha transparency cannot be used together with gradient fills, which is a technical limitation to take note.

Code: after_stat(x) is the modern replacement for the deprecated stat(x) syntax used in the course notes. Both map the computed x-axis value onto the fill aesthetic.

Mapping the probabilities directly onto colour

Beside providing additional geom objects to support the need to plot ridgeline plot, ggridges package also provides a stat function called stat_density_ridges() that replaces stat_density() of ggplot2.

  • The plot
  • The code
ggplot(exam,
       aes(x    = SCIENCE,
           y    = CLASS,
           fill = 0.5 - abs(0.5 - after_stat(ecdf)))) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom      = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    name      = "Tail\nProbability",
    direction = -1
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "Science Score", expand = c(0, 0)) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = NULL, expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))) +
  theme_ridges()

Takeaway: The expression 0.5 - abs(0.5 - ecdf) maps values closest to the median to the highest fill value, and tail extremes to the lowest. Dark regions are the rare extreme scores. Bright central bands show where most students fall. This is especially useful for spotting which classes have fat tails (more outliers) vs tight central distributions.

Important

Always include calc_ecdf = TRUE inside stat_density_ridges() when using ECDF-based colour mapping, the plot will silently produce incorrect results without it.

Ridgeline plots with quantile lines

We can colour the ridgeline by quartile bands to make Q1, median, and Q3 immediately visible within each class’s density shape.

  • Quartile Band
  • The code
  • Probability Band
  • The code
ggplot(exam,
       aes(x    = ENGLISH,
           y    = CLASS,
           fill = factor(after_stat(quantile)))) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom           = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf      = TRUE,
    quantiles      = 4,
    quantile_lines = TRUE
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(name = "Quartiles") +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "English Score", expand = c(0, 0)) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = NULL, expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))) +
  theme_ridges()
ggplot(exam,
       aes(x    = ENGLISH,
           y    = CLASS,
           fill = factor(after_stat(quantile)))) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom      = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE,
    quantiles = c(0.025, 0.975)
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name   = "Probability",
    values = c("#FF0000A0", "#A0A0A0A0", "#0000FFA0"),
    labels = c("Bottom 2.5%", "Middle 95%", "Top 2.5%")
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "English Score", expand = c(0, 0)) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = NULL, expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))) +
  theme_ridges()
